results.possibleIdentity: Gilberto Gerald (born November 27, 1950), also known as Gil Gerald, is an Afro-Panamanian architect, activist, and essayist who was active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. He moved from Panama to the United States in 1967 and studied architecture at the Pratt Institute in Brooklyn, New York, graduating in 1974. During his time at Pratt (1968–1974), he was involved in student leadership and came out as gay, later becoming a prominent figure in the LGBTQ+ and HIV/AIDS activism movements. Professionally trained as an architect, he worked in Washington, D.C., and later co-founded the National Coalition of Black Gays (later the National Coalition of Black Lesbians and Gays, or NCBLG) in 1978. As a writer and essayist, his work has been featured in influential anthologies such as 'In the Life: A Black Gay Anthology' (1986). His association with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) is plausible given his architectural training at Pratt Institute during the peak years of E.A.T.'s activity in New York City (late 1960s to early 1970s), a period when the organization frequently facilitated collaborations between architects, artists, and engineers.

results.possibleIdentity: Salle Werner Vaughn (born 1939) is an American surrealist artist, painter, and installation artist based in Houston, Texas. Active since the late 1960s, she was a participant in the experimental art and music scene in Dallas and Houston. During the late 1960s and early 1970s, her Dallas studio served as a significant meeting place and collaborative space for avant-garde artists and composers, most notably the experimental composer Jerry Hunt, who was known for his work with electronic and mechanical technology. Werner Vaughn contributed to the visual identity of Hunt's projects, including creating cover art and fine-art prints for his Irida record label to support the production of experimental music. Her work from this period often featured geometric and architectural abstractions. In 1973, she exhibited in the Whitney Biennial under the name Salle Werner, where she presented a work titled 'Bound Grid' made of wood and twine. She also participated in the International Women's Year Exhibition at the Contemporary Arts Museum Houston in 1975. Later in her career, she became well-known for 'Harmonium,' a large-scale environmental art installation in Houston consisting of several early 20th-century cottages she transformed into immersive 'dreamscapes.' Her artwork is held in the permanent collections of the Metropolitan Museum of Art in New York and the Museum of Fine Arts, Houston.

results.possibleIdentity: Michael William "Mike" Hart (1943–2016) was an English singer-songwriter, poet, and performer active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. He was a prominent figure in the Liverpool arts scene, initially fronting the rhythm and blues band The Roadrunners before co-founding the influential collective "The Liverpool Scene" in 1967. This group combined poetry, music, and performance art, collaborating with poets such as Adrian Henri and Roger McGough. Hart was known for his raw, eccentric style and recorded several solo albums on John Peel's Dandelion Records label, including 'Mike Hart Bleeds' (1969) and 'Basher, Chalky, Pongo and Me' (1972). His work was part of a broader "total art" movement in Liverpool that blended different artistic disciplines, a spirit similar to the collaborative goals of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization.

Michael Stern Hart (1947–2011) was an American author, technologist, and futurist who pioneered the electronic book (e-book) in 1971. While a student at the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign, he was granted access to the campus's Xerox Sigma V mainframe computer and used his time to digitize the U.S. Declaration of Independence, which he distributed over the ARPANET. This act led to the founding of Project Gutenberg, the world's first digital library. Described as a "cyber-hippie" and a lifelong tinkerer with human-machine interfaces, Hart's work is often cited in the history of digital media and computer art (e.g., in the Johns Hopkins Guide to Digital Media) as a landmark experiment in utilizing technology for literary and artistic dissemination, aligning with the interdisciplinary goals of early art-and-technology collaborations.

Mike Hart was a British musician active in the early 1970s as a member of the instrumental ensemble Continuum. Formed in late 1969 by Hungarian painter and multi-instrumentalist Yoel Schwarcz, the group featured Hart on double bass and John Warren on classical guitar. Continuum was noted for its cross-disciplinary approach, merging classical forms with folk modality and chamber-rock experimentation. The group released two albums on RCA Victor, 'Continuum' (1970) and 'Autumn Grass' (1971), which often utilized electronic textures and contrapuntal arrangements, reflecting the era's trend of exploring technical and artistic boundaries in sound.
